coredata.dc:title: Enhancing detectivity of organic photodetectors through the biomass carbon quantum dots from sugarcane bagasse
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Zhiqiang
Wu, Cheng
Yan, Miao
Yu, Xuan
Yu, Xiaoming
Qian, Qiao
Zhou, Yingtang
zhang, Hai
Li, Zhenhua
Long, Guankui
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, organic photodetectors (OPDs) have received sustained attention due to their tunable wavelength detection, low-cost manufacturing and compatibility with flexible devices. However, as the preparation process proceeds, organic materials inevitably produce some toxic by-products, which triggers an urgent need for green non-toxic optical materials. This paper reports a carbon quantum dot (CQDs) extracted from the green precursor sugarcane bagasse as the sole carbon source, and successfully applied in the OPDs with a structure of FTO/ZnO:CQDs/P3HT/MoO3/Ag for the first time. After CQDs incorporation, the surface of the ZnO thin films exhibits an increased density of oxygen vacancies and adsorbed oxygen. The resulting OPDs exhibit a high detectivity of 2.46 × 1011 Jones and a low noise equivalent power of 1.15 × 10−12 W @ 525 nm. The facile preparation method of CQDs offers a promising opportunity for the green and sustainable synthesis of advanced functional nanomaterials, and the successful demonstration paves new way for the advancement of high-performance OPDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Organic photodetectors
Carbon quantum dots
Sugarcane bagasse
ZnO
Dark current

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Preparation process of CQDs solution. (b) Preparation process of photodetectors.
(a) TEM image of CQDs. (b) SEM top view of ZnO NRs. (c) SEM top view CZnO NRs. (d) Mapping of CZnO NRs. (e) SEM cross-sectional views of ZnO NRs. (f) SEM cross-sectional views of CZnO NRs.

(a) UV–visible absorption spectra (Insets show photographs of the CQDs in aqueous solution under daylight (left) and UV irradiation (right). (b) XRD patterns of samples. (c) XPS full spectrum of ZnO NRs, CZnO NRs. (d) Zn 2p of ZnO NRs, CZnO NRs. (e) O 1s, of ZnO NRs, CZnO NRs. (f) C 1s, of ZnO NRs, CZnO NRs.

(a) UV–visible absorption spectra. (b) (ahv)
2
–hv function curve obtained from absorption. (c) Nyquist plot (fitted curve) and equivalent circuit diagram of samples. (d) Dark I–V characteristics of devices. (e) I–V characteristics of devices under UV (10 mW cm−2). (f) I–V characteristics of devices under Visible (10 mW cm−2). (g) Responsivity, (h) Normalized detectability and (i) Noise equivalent power of the devices with ZnO NRs (0), CZnO NRs-1 (1), CZnO NRs-2 (2) and CZnO NRs-3 (3), respectively.
(a) Schematic diagram of CZnO NRs in dark condition. (b) Schematic diagram of CZnO NRs in light condition.
Comparison table of performance indexes of photodetectors based on quantum dots or nanoparticles.

head.ce:title: Enhancing detectivity of organic photodetectors through the biomass carbon quantum dots from sugarcane bagasse

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, organic photodetectors (OPDs) have received sustained attention due to their tunable wavelength detection, low-cost manufacturing and compatibility with flexible devices. However, as the preparation process proceeds, organic materials inevitably produce some toxic by-products, which triggers an urgent need for green non-toxic optical materials. This paper reports a carbon quantum dot (CQDs) extracted from the green precursor sugarcane bagasse as the sole carbon source, and successfully applied in the OPDs with a structure of FTO/ZnO:CQDs/P3HT/MoO3/Ag for the first time. After CQDs incorporation, the surface of the ZnO thin films exhibits an increased density of oxygen vacancies and adsorbed oxygen. The resulting OPDs exhibit a high detectivity of 2.46 × 1011 Jones and a low noise equivalent power of 1.15 × 10−12 W @ 525 nm. The facile preparation method of CQDs offers a promising opportunity for the green and sustainable synthesis of advanced functional nanomaterials, and the successful demonstration paves new way for the advancement of high-performance OPDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic photodetectors (OPDs) have significant advantages such as cost-effective thin film processing, low-cost solution manufacturing capabilities, and ease of portability and flexibility, which have attracted widespread interest from both academia and industry for various sensing applications [1–6]. Organic-inorganic heterojunctions typically generate photovoltaic effects through the staggered energy band alignment between p-type organic semiconductor materials and n-type inorganic semiconductor materials. This greatly enhances the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and helps to improve the self-powering performance. ZnO is typically one of the preferred n-type inorganic semiconductor materials for ultraviolet detectors [7–9]. Furthermore, ZnO is characterized as a semiconductor with a wide direct bandgap of 3.37 eV and a significant exciton binding energy of 60 meV, making it a highly promising contender in the field of optoelectronic detectors [10–14]. The existence of p-n heterojunctions between organic-inorganic heterojunction semiconductors can improve photoresponsivity [15,16].

The good crystalline properties, controllable optoelectronic characteristics, and large surface area of ZnO nanorods (NRs) make them particularly attractive, providing high carrier mobility and long diffusion lengths, as compared to ZnO nanoparticles [17] and other nanostructure of ZnO thin films. Studies indicated that ZnO NRs are a good contestant for OPDs can be attributed to the following reasons: Firstly, ZnO NR may provide direct channels for carriers thus shorten the average transport distance and suppresses the recombination [18,19]. Secondly, it can feature higher electron mobilities for organic device [19]. Thirdly, ZnO NRs can provide light trapping and increase light absorption [20]. However, when combined with certain p-type semiconductors, ZnO NRs exhibit poor detection performance due to severe photogenerated carrier recombination caused by large band offsets and poor interface material quality [21]. Up to now, some researchers have attempted to improve the performance of ZnO NRs by doping with metal elements. For example, Weng et al. [22] doped ZnO NRs with Mg and Al elements to enhance the light absorption of the devices, achieving a detectivity of 4.35 × 109 Jones and a linear dynamic region of 21.56 dB (25 mA cm−2, 625 nm). While the attachment of metal compounds can enhance the light absorption of ZnO NRs, their potential toxicity raises concerns for their practical use [23].

CQDs have garnered significant attention in recent years owing to their low toxicity [24], environmentally friendly synthesis methods [25], chemical stability [26], and tunable photoluminescence [27]. Research has shown that the combination of carbon dots with semiconductor detector materials can significantly improve the optoelectronic conversion efficiency of the detectors [28,29]. Previous studies have shown that CQDs can improve the surface defects of ZnO nanoparticles and enhance carrier separation at the CQDs/ZnO nanoparticle interface [30]. For instance, fabricated a flexible self-powered ultraviolet photodetector by modifying ZnO NRs with CQDs, which exhibited a responsivity of 22.62 mA W−1 and D* 5.80 × 1011 Jones [31]. The rise and fall times of the UV photodetector is 0.09 s/0.12 s at a light intensity of 0.32 mW cm−2.

Recent studies have shown that many “green” materials can be used as carbon sources for synthesizing CQDs and are clearly non-toxic. For example, Lu et al. synthesized blue-emitting CQDs for detecting Hg2+ using pomelo peel as the carbon source [3]. Sahu et al. reported the one-step synthesis of highly luminescent CQDs by hydrothermal treatment of orange juice in ethanol [32]. Li et al. prepared CQDs for accelerated wound healing by microwave-assisted hydrothermal method using ginger as the only carbon precursor [33]. However, there is currently limited research on the application of biomass CQDs in OPDs [34]. Therefore, we selected sugarcane bagasse as a carbon source to synthesize CQDs and employed them in OPDs. Sugarcane bagasse is the fibrous lignocellulosic residue of sugarcane after it has been crushed for the extraction of its juice used for sugar and ethanol production. It is composed mainly of cellulose, hemicellulose and lignin, with a high degree of lignification and low protein, starch and soluble sugar content [35]. The use of sugarcane bagasse as a source for the preparation of carbon materials has been reported. For instance, Nieto-Delgado [36] and Purnomo [37] used sugarcane bagasse to prepare activated carbon, and Si et al. prepared activated carbon from sugarcane bagasse and applied it to supercapacitors [38].

In this study, we report the synthesis of pale-yellow CQDs solution using a one-pot hydrothermal method, utilizing waste sugarcane bagasse as the precursor. Subsequently, we applied these CQDs in OPDs and achieved favorable outcomes. Due to surface oxygen defects and an increase in adsorbed oxygen density, the depletion zone widens and the barrier height increases. Therefore, the dark current significantly decreases by two orders of magnitude. Furthermore, the device exhibits excellent normalized detection rate and noise equivalent power at 0 V bias, with respective values of 2.46 × 1011 Jones and 1.15 × 10−12 W, which is approximately 2.5 times higher than the original device.

Sugarcane bagasse was utilized as a single carbon precursor to synthesize CQDs through a hydrothermal method. First, 1 g of ground bagasse was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave along with 30 ml of deionized water. It was then subjected to hydrothermal treatment in an oven at 180 °C for 12 h. The autoclave was allowed to cool to room temperature, then opened to obtain a brownish solution. The solution was sonicated for 30 min and then centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 10 min to extract the upper clear layer. The solution was then filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and dialyzed in deionized water with a dialysis bag of molecular weight 500 for 24 h to remove inorganic ions and other possible impurities. The final solution is a light-yellow solution of CQDs that emits a blue color under UV light (Fig. S1(a)), as shown in Fig. 1 a.

The FTO glass substrate was cleaned sequentially with glass cleaner, ethanol, and deionized water for 15 min, respectively. The ZnO seed layer was spin-coated onto the cleaned FTO glass substrate using a sol-gel method. The sol–gel solution of ZnO seed layer was prepared from zinc acetate dihydrate and ethanolamine in ethanol, which can reference our previous work [39]. The sample was annealed at 240 °C in air for 40 min, then followed by a 1 h annealing at 380 °C.

The CQDs doped ZnO NRs (short for CZnO NRs) thin films were prepared based on the ZnO seed layer by hydrothermal synthesis. The solution reagents included zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O), hexamethylenetetramine (HMT) and the CQDs solution. The samples mixed with the solution and were placed into a stainless-steel autoclave lined with a polytetrafluoroethylene liner, then kept at 95 °C for 90 min. After cooling down to room temperature, the sample was washed with deionized water. The devices mixed with different contents of CQDs solutions were divided into CZnO NRs-1 (10 ml), CZnO NRs-2 (15 ml), and CZnO NRs-3 (20 ml), respectively. The front view of the device is shown in Fig. S1 (b).

After the preparation of FTO/CZnO NRs, an active layer of poly (3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT) chlorobenzene solution (15 mg/ml) was spin-coated. And then the samples with active layer were annealed at 120 °C under nitrogen atmosphere for 10 min. Finally, the samples were vapor coated with MoO3 (3 nm) and Ag (120 nm) under vacuum (10−4 Pa), the spec specific preparation process is shown in Fig. 1b.

The morphology and structure of the as-prepared materials were characterized by SEM (SUPRA 55 V P). The microstructures of CQDs were investigated with transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100 F). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) (DX2700) patterns of ZnO NRs/CZnO NRs film were measured in 20°–70° accordance. The optical transmission and absorption spectrum were measured in 200–600 nm accordance with an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2600). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermofisher Nexsa) was employed in determination of the chemical properties of the film. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed on the films using an electrochemical analyzer (CHI660E). The device was characterized using a Keithley 2400 source measure unit. The current density-voltage (I–V) characteristics of the device were tested at 360 nm, 380 nm, 400 nm, 460 nm, 525 nm and 660 nm under light intensity irradiation of 10 mw/cm2.

Initially, an initial investigation was conducted employing TEM to evaluate the size properties of CQDs, as visually depicted in Fig. 2 a. By analyzing over 120 particles from various TEM images, histograms were generated. The TEM images, accompanied by the corresponding size histograms, demonstrate the sphericity and excellent dispersion of the CQDs. Their sizes range from 1.5 to 5.5 nm in diameter, with an average diameter of 3.20 nm. Notably, the CQDs exhibit uniform dispersion without any noticeable aggregation.

SEM views of the thin films with and without CQDs modified layer (CZnO NRs-2) are shown in Fig. 2b-c. It can be seen that the ZnO NRs grown by hydrothermal method are highly ordered and perpendicular to the FTO conductive glass substrate. Among them, the average length of ZnO NRs is about 340 nm and the diameter is about 40 nm. And this high-density nanoarray helps to amplify the photogenerated current. After doping with CQDs, the CZnO NRs have a length of about 180 nm and a diameter of about 75 nm. It can be seen from the figure that the doping of CQDs changes the surface morphology of the nanorods. This may be due to possible surface interactions between CQDs and ZnO NRs, resulting in influencing the growth kinetics of ZnO NRs and leading to changes in size and morphology. The cross-sectional views of these two films are shown in Fig. 2e-f. Fig. 2d and Fig. S2 show the Mapping and EDS diagram of CZnO NRs composite, respectively. The highest content of element Zn is 58.74%, followed by element O at 29.21% and finally element C at 12.05%. From the uniform distribution of element C in the Mapping diagram it can be judged that CQDs are uniformly distributed over the ZnO NRs.

The tunable fluorescence properties of CQDs have garnered significant interest among researchers. In this study, we conducted measurements to assess the absorption characteristics of sugarcane bagasse-derived CQDs solution within the wavelength range of 200–600 nm, as depicted in Fig. 3 a. The UV spectrum of the CQDs exhibited a distinctive absorption peak at 222 nm, which can be attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 hybridization. The CQDs solution appeared yellowish under visible light. However, upon exposure to long-wavelength UV light (365 nm), the solution transformed into an intensely vibrant blue luminescent state (insets of Fig. 3a).

The XRD patterns of the CZnO NRs are shown in Fig. 3b–and a comparison is made with the standard reference card for ZnO (PDF#36–1451). In four samples, the (002) diffraction peak of the hexagonal fibrillated ZnO structure appears at approximately 34.4° [40]. The (002) peak decreases continuously with increasing volume of doped CQDs solution, and CZnO NRs-3 exhibits one of the lowest (002) peaks. The decrease of the (002) peak may be caused by the interaction of CQDs and ZnO seed layer changing the morphology of the nanorods. No significant signal of CQDs was detected in the XRD pattern, which may be due to the low content of CQDs. The intensity of the (002) diffraction peak indicates that ZnO is growing in a direction that is perpendicular to the C-axis of the FTO substrate. SEM analysis results support this conclusion. The XRD patterns of CQDs showed broad-band diffraction peaks at 2θ = 22° for CQDs (Fig. S2b), indicating the amorphous carbon phase of the CQDs.

To gain further insight into the interaction between ZnO NRs and CQDs, XPS spectra with carbon (C 1s = 284.8 eV) calibration were acquired and are depicted in Fig. 3(c–f). The overall spectrum shown in Fig. 3c confirms the presence of four elements in the CZnO NRs composite, namely zinc (Zn 2p), oxygen (O 1s), and carbon (C 1s). As shown in Fig. 3d, the Zn 2p spectrum of CZnO NRs displays two symmetrical peaks at 1021.88 eV and 1044.93 eV, which correspond to the Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 of ZnO, respectively. The Zn 2p spectrum of the composite material has shifted towards higher binding energy by 0.15 eV in comparison to that of pure ZnO NRs, which is attributed to the interaction between ZnO NRs and CQDs. The peak observed at 284.78 eV in Fig. 3f corresponds to the C–C bond in the sp2 orbitals, while the peaks at 286.48 eV and 288.53 eV correspond to the C–O and CO bonds, respectively [41,42], indicating the presence of carbon-oxygen bonds in the composites. The O 1s spectrum of the ZnO NRs depicted in Fig. 3e exhibits three distinct peaks, with peak centers located at 530.17, 532.03, and 533.13 eV, respectively. These peaks can be attributed to lattice oxygen, defect oxygen, and surface adsorbed oxygen associated with the ZnO NRs [43,44]. In the O 1s spectrum of CZnO NRs, the peak corresponding to lattice oxygen in ZnO NRs undergoes a shift from 530.17 eV to 530.43 eV, accompanied by a notable enhancement in peak intensity. This observation suggests the existence of Zn–COO bonds [3,45], which can be attributed to the incorporation of carboxyl groups from CQDs into the lattice oxygen sites of ZnO NRs. As a result, the peak corresponding to defect oxygen in CZnO NRs shifts from 532.03 eV to 532.13 eV, accompanied by a noticeable increase in peak area. This signifies the generation of more oxygen vacancies in CZnO NRs, providing further evidence of the interaction between ZnO NRs and CQDs. Moreover, the peak associated with surface adsorbed oxygen in ZnO NRs shifted from 533.13 to 533.18 eV and was accompanied by an increase in peak intensity. This suggests that the incorporation of CQDs leads to an enhanced adsorption of atmospheric oxygen atoms onto the surface of ZnO NRs.

We proceeded to characterize the optical properties of the ZnO NRs and CZnO NRs films. Fig. 4 a shows the light transmission of CZnO NRs films, which exhibits a slight increase in the range of 360–560 nm compared to ZnO NRs films. This slight increase can be attributed to the doping of CQDs, which alters the surface morphology of the ZnO NRs. On the other hand, the light absorption of CZnO NRs films in the range of 300–360 nm is significantly higher than that of ZnO NRs films. This can be attributed to the strong light absorption capability of CQDs in the UV region. Notably, the absorption of CZnO NRs-3 films is significantly lower than that of samples 1 and 2. This can be explained by the fact that ZnO NRs have the ability to absorb ultraviolet light [46], and the higher content of CQDs in CZnO NRs-3 thin film composite materials leads to competition for ultraviolet light absorption. These results indicate that further increasing the content of CQDs may result in a significant decrease in absorption efficiency. The optical bandgap of the thin film was determined from the absorption curve using the Tauc formula:
(1)
(
α h υ
)
1 n
= Α
(
h ν −
E g
)
where
α
is the absorption coefficient,
h
is the Planck constant,
ν
is the frequency, A is a constant, and
E g
is the bandgap of the semiconductor. Since ZnO is a direct band gap semiconductor, n takes the value of 1/2. As the amount of CQDs increases, the strong electronegativity mismatch between atoms and the appearance of impurity levels in ZnO caused the optical bandgap to decrease from 4.11 eV (ZnO NRs) to 4.01 eV (CZnO NRs-2) (Fig. 4b) [3,47]. This suggests that CQDs can enhance light absorption and improve light utilization.

In order to further investigate the interaction between CQDs and ZnO NRs, we employed electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) to characterize the conductivity of the device. Fig. 4c illustrates the equivalent circuit used, which consists of series resistance (Rs), charge transfer resistance (Rct), and parallel capacitance (C). The increase in Rct from approximately 50,000 (ZnO NRs) to approximately 100,000 (CZnO NRs-2) indicates a decrease in the conductivity of the composite film. Interestingly, the Rct of CZnO NRs-3, which has the highest CQDs doping level, is approximately 60,000, smaller than that of CZnO NRs-2. This suggests that the doping of a sufficient amount of CQDs may contribute to partial conductivity due to the good electrical conductivity of CQDs. When considering the SEM and XPS findings, the decrease in conductivity can likely be attributed to changes in the nanorods' morphology and an increase in oxygen defects within the composite film. The presence of oxygen defects introduces scattering centers for charge carriers, which hinders the flow of charge carriers and decreases the carrier mobility, thereby reducing the electrical conductivity of the material. Which are in agreement with the XPS results. Recently, Y. J. Park et al. reported the fabrication of hybridization between graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and ZnO nanoflowers (NFs) for mediating photoluminescence [48], the increased defective states on the surface of ZnO had also been demonstrated, from the strong and broad photoluminescence (PL) emission from near UV to red region.

Subsequently, we conducted I–V testing on the device in a dark environment. Fig. 4d demonstrates that the devices exhibit favorable rectification properties. ZnO NRs displayed the highest dark current, measuring 3.87 × 10−3 mA at 0 V bias. However, with the introduction of carbon dots doping, CZnO NRs-2 exhibited the lowest dark current, measuring 7.24 × 10−5 mA. The dark current decreased by two orders of magnitude compared to ZnO NRs.

We conducted I-T response curve testing of the device under UV and visible light using a light source meter, as depicted in Fig. 4e-f. The magnitude of the photocurrent diminishes progressively with the incorporation of CQDs. Specifically, ZnO NRs demonstrate the highest photocurrent intensity (13.85 mA), whereas CZnO NRs-3 exhibit the lowest photocurrent (1.98 mA). Both samples reach their maximum photocurrent at 525 nm, which can be attributed to the optimal absorption of green visible light by P3HT [49]. Simultaneously, along with the decrease in photocurrent, the dark current also undergoes a substantial reduction by a factor of two orders of magnitude, as shown in Fig. S3. It is important to highlight that the CZnO NRs-2 device demonstrates the lowest dark current, contrary to expectations, as CZnO NRs-3, which was doped with a higher concentration of CQDs. In the context of photodetectors, both dark current and photocurrent play indispensable roles. In photodetectors, both dark and light currents play crucial roles.

To further evaluate the device performance, we calculated and analyzed the important parameters of the photodetector responsivity (R), the normalized detectivity (D*) and noise equivalent power (NEP). They are calculated by the following equations [50]:
(2)
R =
I
p h
P
i l l
=
I
p h o t o
−
I
d a r k
S ×
I
i l l
(3)
D *
=
R ×
S
i
n o i s e
=
R ×
S
(
2 q ×
I
d a r k
)
(4)
N E P =
i
n o i s e
R
where
I
p h o t o
and
I
d a r k
represent the photocurrent and dark current of the PDs, respectively.
I
i l l
is the incident light power at 525 nm with a power density of 10 mW cm−2,
S
is the effective area of the device (0.08 cm2),
q
is the actual charge 1.6 × 10−19 C. The decline in responsivity shown in Fig. 4g (Samples 0–3 are ZnO NRs, CZnO NRs-1, CZnO NRs-2 and CZnO NRs-3, respectively) is due to the lower value of
I
p h
(
I
p h
=
I
p h o t o
−
I
d a r k
) in the device. The parameters of NEP and D* are crucial for assessing the ability of the OPDs to detect weak signals. The results indicate that CZnO NRs-2 exhibits a lower NEP value and a higher D*, as illustrated in Fig. 4h-i. In comparison to the ZnO NRs device, D* increased from 8.34 × 1010 Jones to 2.46 × 1011 Jones, representing an improvement of approximately 2.95 times. NEP decreased from 3.39 × 10−12 W to 1.15 × 10−12 W, which corresponds to a decrease of about 2.95 times. A superior normalized detectivity necessitates higher responsivity and lower dark current. The OPDs incorporating CQDs demonstrate enhanced normalized detection efficiency and reduced noise equivalent power, indicating that the doping of CQDs can significantly enhance the overall performance of the OPDs.

In addition, the response speed of OPDs under 525 nm light was investigated, by the rise time (τrise, the period of the current increased to Iph/e) and decay time (τdecay, period of the current decreased to Iph × (1−1/e)) [51]. Compared with ZnO NRs devices (Fig. S4a), devices doped with CQDs (Figs. S4b–d) exhibit a slight increase in τrise and τdecay times. As the CQDs content increases, the τrise of the device gradually increased from 29 to 33 ms (CZnO NRs-2), and the τdecay of device gradually increased from 28 to 30 ms (CZnO NRs-3). The incorporation of CQDs in the device leads to an augmentation of oxygen vacancies and absorbed oxygen on the surface of the membrane. As a result, when the device is exposed to incident light, the photo-generated holes are captured by the neutral oxygen vacancies [52]. This interaction between the holes and oxygen vacancies has an influence on the τrise and τdecay of the device. Moreover, the enhanced adsorption of oxygen on the surface of the nanorods leads to an elongated duration for the adsorption and dissociation of oxygen atoms in CZnO NRs. This mechanism will be elaborated in detail in the forthcoming sections.

In comparison to recently reported photodetectors based on quantum dots or nanoparticles, the CZnO NRs-2 device exhibits certain competitive advantages in terms of optoelectronic performance. The specific details and performance metrics are listed in Table 1 [53–57], and additional comprehensive information can be found in Fig. S5. These comparative analyses highlight the strengths and advantages of the CZnO NRs-2 device over other photodetectors based on quantum dots or nanostructures, showcasing its potential as a promising candidate in the field of optoelectronics.

The high surface area and grain boundaries in the NRs structure create numerous trap states on the NRs surface. These surface trap states can easily adsorb oxygen molecules in the dark and capture free electrons. The response mechanism of photosensitive behavior of ZnO NRs can be explained as follows: when the device is exposed to ambient air under dark conditions, oxygen molecules are adsorbed onto the surface and ionized due to their strong electronegativity trapping electrons from ZnO NRs. The reaction process can be expressed by the following equation [52]:
(5)
O
2
( g )
+
e −
→
O
2
(
a d
)
−
When the energy of the incident light is higher than Eg (the bandgap energy), the ZnO NRs array absorbs the energy of the incident photons and generates electron-hole pairs. The photogenerated holes tend to migrate to the surface of ZnO NRs, where they undergo surface electron-hole recombination, releasing the adsorbed O2 from the surface of the nanorods. This reaction reduces the depletion layer width and barrier height of ZnO NRs. The reaction process can be represented by the following equations:
(6)
O
2
(
a d
)
−
+
h +
→
O
2
( g )

Upon the introduction of CQDs, O2 is captured by the CQDs from the ambient air [58], leading to its adsorption on the surface of ZnO NRs. This process increases the depletion width of ZnO NRs and elevates the barrier height on the nanorod surface, consequently reducing the conductivity of ZnO NRs, as shown in Fig. 5 a. As a result, the dark current of the photodetector will be reduced. Under the illumination of incident light, the bandgap reduction in the CZnO NRs thin film enables it to absorb more energy from photons, resulting in the generation of a higher number of electron-hole pairs and subsequent release of the adsorbed O2 on the nanorod surface, as illustrated in Fig. 5b. This process leads to a decrease in the depletion layer width and barrier height of ZnO NRs, thereby increasing the photocurrent of the photodetector.

In summary, we reported a kind of green biomass CQDs extracted from wasted sugarcane bagasse and applied it to OPDs by a simple method. The optimized OPDs after doping CQDs changed the surface morphology of ZnO NRs, reduced the band gap and improved the utilization of incident light. The augmentation of surface oxygen defects and the density of adsorbed oxygen leads to the broadening of the depletion region and an elevation in the barrier height. As a consequence, the dark current exhibits a notable reduction by two orders of magnitude. Among them, CZnO NRs-2 exhibited the best performance, with the highest detectivity of 2.46 × 1011 Jones and the lowest NEP of 1.15 × 10−12 W. Compared with the original device, the performance was improved by about 2.95 times. The results indicate that CQDs have good feasibility and promising applications in optoelectronics and sensing. And it provides a possibility for the further development of low-cost OPDs.

Zhiqiang Luo: Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Cheng Wu: Data curation, Investigation. Miao Yan: Investigation, Methodology. Xuan Yu: Project administration, Supervision, W. Xiaoming Yu: Methodology, Supervision. Qiao Qian: Formal analysis, Software. Yingtang Zhou: Methodology, Resources. Hai zhang: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition. Zhenhua Li: Conceptualization, Visualization. Guankui Long: Methodology, Validation, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 11604298, 52103218, 92256202, 12261131500), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation (LY19F050007), Zhoushan Science and Technology Project (2019C21029, 2019C21017, 2023C41021), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, Nankai University (Grant Number: 023-63223021).
